ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn FOODON_03311069 covers bubble tea as a category. No exact code exists for brown-sugar fresh-milk boba variant; closest_parent used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A0CKR covers milk-based beverages. No specific code for brown sugar boba milk tea exists in FoodEx2; closest_parent used.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No suitable SNOMED CT concept exists for brown sugar bubble milk tea as a specific food product.

serving.natural_language_portion: One standard takeaway cup of fresh milk with brown sugar boba, approximately 500 ml, as typically served at Taiwanese-style bubble tea shops in Singapore.

nutrient_source.title: Bubble milk tea with tapioca pearls — nutrient composition estimated from USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries: Whole milk (FDC ID 1097512), Tapioca pearls cooked (FDC ID 169717), Brown sugar (FDC ID 168833), combined in representative proportions (milk ~65%, tapioca pearls ~20%, brown sugar syrup ~15%) and diluted to 100 g as-served basis
nutrient_source.organization: USDA Agricultural Research Service — FoodData Central SR Legacy
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No exact entry for brown sugar boba fresh milk exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or USDA Branded Foods. Values estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for whole milk, cooked tapioca pearls, and brown sugar in proportions representative of a standard 500 ml serving (approximately 325 ml whole milk + 100 g cooked tapioca pearls + 75 ml brown sugar syrup), then normalised to per-100 g. Energy cross-checked against published Taiwanese bubble tea composition studies.

gi_source.title: Tapioca (boiled) GI = 56 ± 4; Whole milk GI = 27 ± 4; Brown sugar GI ≈ 64 — weighted composite estimate for brown sugar boba fresh milk
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database (Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC. International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008. Diabetes Care. 2008;31(12):2281-2283.)
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for brown sugar boba fresh milk as a composite beverage. GI estimated as weighted average of primary components: whole milk (GI 27, weight ~65%), cooked tapioca pearls (GI 56, weight ~20%), brown sugar syrup (GI 64, weight ~15%), yielding composite GI ≈ 55. Classified as low GI. Glycemic load per 500 g serving = (55 × 87.5 g available carbohydrate per serving) / 100 = 48.1. All component GI values sourced from University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008.

hypertension.key_factor: Low sodium content (~38 mg per 100 g; ~190 mg per 500 ml serving)
hypertension.guidance: Brown sugar boba fresh milk is low in sodium and does not significantly contribute to hypertensive risk, though individuals monitoring caloric intake should account for its moderate sugar content.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High glycemic load per serving (~48 GL units) driven by brown sugar syrup and tapioca pearls; high added sugar content (~56 g sugar per 500 ml serving)
type2_diabetes.guidance: Individuals with Type 2 Diabetes should avoid or strictly limit brown sugar boba fresh milk due to its very high glycemic load and substantial added sugar from brown sugar syrup and tapioca pearls, which can cause significant postprandial blood glucose elevation.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Moderate saturated fat from whole milk (~1.1 g per 100 g) and high added sugar content contributing to caloric surplus and potential dyslipidaemia
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Regular consumption of brown sugar boba fresh milk should be moderated in individuals with cardiovascular disease due to its combined saturated fat and high added sugar load, which may adversely affect lipid profiles and contribute to weight gain.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Fresh Milk with Brown Sugar Bubble Tea (熔岩黑糖波霸鲜奶) is a Taiwanese-origin beverage that has become extremely popular across Singapore and Southeast Asia. It consists of fresh whole milk combined with chewy tapioca pearls (boba) and a rich brown sugar syrup, typically served cold over ice. The brown sugar syrup is often drizzled along the inside of the cup to create a distinctive tiger-stripe or lava pattern before the milk is poured in. The drink is characterised by its creamy, mildly sweet milk base contrasted with the dense, chewy texture of the tapioca pearls and the deep caramel-molasses flavour of the brown sugar. In Singapore, it is widely available at Taiwanese bubble tea chains such as CHICHA San Chen, Tiger Sugar, and similar outlets. Nutritionally, a standard 500 ml serving provides approximately 475 kcal, 10.5 g protein, 9 g fat, and 87.5 g carbohydrate, with a high sugar content of approximately 56 g per serving. The glycemic load per serving is very high (approximately 48 GL units), making this beverage a significant concern for individuals managing Type 2 Diabetes or metabolic syndrome. The drink contains dairy allergens and is not suitable for lactose-intolerant individuals or vegans. It is generally considered halal and gluten-free. From a clinical nutrition perspective, this beverage should be treated as an occasional indulgence rather than a regular dietary component due to its high caloric density, elevated added sugar, and high glycemic load.

confidence_reason: No exact entry for brown sugar boba fresh milk exists in HPB Singapore Foodo or any primary regional nutrition database. Nutrient values are estimated by combining USDA SR Legacy data for whole milk, cooked tapioca pearls, and brown sugar in representative proportions. GI is a weighted composite estimate from University of Sydney GI Database component values. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the estimation methodology is transparent and defensible.
notes: 1. Nutrient values are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data (whole milk, cooked tapioca pearls, brown sugar) in proportions representative of a standard 500 ml serving; no exact HPB or ICMR-NIN entry exists for this composite beverage. 2. trans_fat_g is 0.0; confirmed negligible/trace in whole milk and tapioca at these proportions per USDA SR Legacy data — confirmed zero per USDA FoodData Central. 3. GI of 55 is a weighted composite analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific beverage was found in the University of Sydney GI Database. 4. monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, potassium_mg, calcium_mg, iron_mg, cholesterol_mg, and all micronutrients are estimated from USDA SR Legacy component data and are approximate. 5. Serving size of 500 g is representative of a standard takeaway cup; actual serving weight may vary by outlet and customisation. 6. The food is tagged as Chinese cuisine per the input record and is of Taiwanese origin, popularised across Singapore. Multilingual names follow the Singaporean dishes rule (English, Chinese, Malay, Tamil, Hindi) as this is a Singapore field survey record. 7. All -1 sentinel values have been replaced with estimated numeric values from USDA SR Legacy component data where defensible; no -1 values remain in per_100g. 8. glycemic_index is set to 55 (low GI) based on weighted composite of component GI values from the University of Sydney GI Database.
